techniques.evidence_quote: These cloud credentials allowed the threat actors to gain access to the AWS account where this IAM user resided and perform AWS application program interface (API) calls.

the threat actor used an automated script with the AWS CLI and attempted to create new S3 buckets. These buckets had various name variations of contact-shinycorp-tutanota-com-# with the # replaced with ascending numbers... All the buckets were created within ten minutes of starting the CreateBucket operations.

the threat actors obtained AWS credentials from a sensitive file exposed on the internet. The file contained a variety of credentials, but the group specifically targeted the exposed AWS access key belonging to an identity and access management (IAM) user

Against the IAM service, the threat actor performed ListUsers, which returns a list of the existing users within the AWS account. Due to the limited permissions associated with the access key, the API call failed.

the group performed the ListBuckets API call using the AWS Command Line Interface (CLI)... the threat actors switched to using the S3 Browser tool to iterate through the S3 buckets in the account and that generated both GetBucketLocation and GetBucketObjectLockConfiguration events in the CloudTrail logs.

the threat actor group used WinSCP to graphically view all the S3 buckets in the account... The ransom note later sent by the threat actor provided proof of data access, but it did not provide enough specifics to determine what S3 data left the environment.
the threat actor deleted a handful of buckets, which resulted in the DeleteBucket API call.

they completed their attack by sending an extortion email to the victim organization. The note stated the group had shifted to extortion to make more money and that the victim organization had one week to pay

attack_chain.description: Bling Libra obtained an exposed AWS IAM access key and secret from a sensitive file publicly accessible on the internet (e.g., a public code repository or similar exposed resource).
attack_chain.evidence_quote: the threat actors obtained AWS credentials from a sensitive file exposed on the internet. The file contained a variety of credentials, but the group specifically targeted the exposed AWS access key belonging to an identity and access management (IAM) user

attack_chain.description: Using the stolen IAM access key and secret, the threat actors authenticated to the victim's AWS account and began making API calls, leveraging the AmazonS3FullAccess policy attached to the compromised IAM user.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: These cloud credentials allowed the threat actors to gain access to the AWS account where this IAM user resided and perform AWS application program interface (API) calls.

attack_chain.description: The threat actor attempted to enumerate IAM users via the ListUsers API call to understand account scope, but the call failed due to insufficient permissions.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: Against the IAM service, the threat actor performed ListUsers, which returns a list of the existing users within the AWS account. Due to the limited permissions associated with the access key, the API call failed.

attack_chain.description: The threat actor used the AWS CLI to execute ListBuckets and then switched to S3 Browser to enumerate all S3 buckets, their locations, and object lock configurations, gaining full visibility into available cloud storage resources.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: the group performed the ListBuckets API call using the AWS Command Line Interface (CLI)... the threat actors switched to using the S3 Browser tool to iterate through the S3 buckets in the account and that generated both GetBucketLocation and GetBucketObjectLockConfiguration events in the CloudTrail logs.

attack_chain.description: After a ~one-month pause, the threat actor returned and used WinSCP to access all S3 buckets and their contents. The ransom note later provided proof of data access, strongly implying data was exfiltrated, though object-level logging was not enabled to confirm it.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: the threat actor group used WinSCP to graphically view all the S3 buckets in the account... The ransom note later sent by the threat actor provided proof of data access, but it did not provide enough specifics to determine what S3 data left the environment.

attack_chain.description: The threat actor deleted a selection of S3 buckets using WinSCP, generating DeleteBucket API calls recorded in CloudTrail.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: the threat actor deleted a handful of buckets, which resulted in the DeleteBucket API call.

attack_chain.technique_name: Command and Scripting Interpreter: Cloud API
attack_chain.description: Following bucket deletion, the threat actor ran an automated AWS CLI script to rapidly create numerous new S3 buckets named 'contact-shinycorp-tutanota-com-#', all created within ten minutes, serving as a taunt and ransom contact method.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: the threat actor used an automated script with the AWS CLI and attempted to create new S3 buckets. These buckets had various name variations of contact-shinycorp-tutanota-com-# with the # replaced with ascending numbers... All the buckets were created within ten minutes of starting the CreateBucket operations.

attack_chain.description: Bling Libra sent an extortion email to the victim demanding payment within one week, threatening to expose stolen data. This marks the group's tactical shift from data resale to direct victim extortion.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: they completed their attack by sending an extortion email to the victim organization. The note stated the group had shifted to extortion to make more money and that the victim organization had one week to pay

initial_access_vector: AWS IAM access key and secret harvested from a sensitive file publicly exposed on the internet
persistence_mechanism: None observed; the threat actor did not establish persistence mechanisms due to limited IAM permissions
privilege_escalation: None observed; the compromised IAM credentials only had AmazonS3FullAccess and attempts to enumerate IAM users failed
